title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 1)
text:
COS-FIELD ISA
Program Review
Submitted By:
Samantha Worthington, FIELD Director
College of the Siskiyous
800 College Avenue
Weed, CA 96094
January 7, 2021

-- 1 of 19 --

1. Program Description:
Since 2012, the College of the Siskiyous has maintained a partnership with
Farmworker Institute for Education and Leadership Development (FIELD)
through an Instructional Service Agreement (ISA). The agreement entered is that
FIELD provides instructional and other services for non-credit English as a
Second Language (ESL) courses and potentially other non-credit courses and
COS provides administrative and academic supervision of instruction carried out
by FIELD employees, record keeping, and other administrative services
pertaining to course offerings. The regulations pertaining to ISAs are contained in
California Education Code sections 78015, 84752 and California Code of
Regulations, title 5, sections 51006, 53410, 55002, 55003, 55005, 55300, 58050,
58051(c)–(g), 58051.5, 58055, 58056, 58058(b), 58102-58106, 58108. In 2017, a
College position was created and filled — FIELD Director — to oversee the ISA.
COS faculty develop curriculum for the courses. The College collects
apportionment on all the classes offered by FIELD and reimburses FIELD
throughout the year based on a mutually agreed upon rate per hour of
instruction.
Over the last four years classes were offered in various locations including Kern,
Monterey, and Los Angeles counties. Classes are brought to students by holding
classrooms in neighborhood community centers, schools, and churches. These
ESL courses serve as an important pathway for FIELD as many of its students
continue on to its high school (mentioned below) to pursue high school diplomas.
The FIELD ISA, bolsters and broadens the College's ability to serve underserved
communities.The agreement is renewed annually. In April 2017, the Director role
was created to ensure increased oversight of the ISA overall after the
accreditation team in 2016 cited some concerns regarding oversight. The
Director — a College employee — serves as the liaison between the College and
FIELD and does not oversee a specific academic program, rather oversees
programming related to the FIELD relationship.

-- 2 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 2)
text:
Founded by Cesar Chavez in 1978, FIELD is a nonprofit organization that
promotes economic and social prosperity for underserved communities through
education, workforce training, and economic development. FIELD delivers its
classes to economically disadvantaged adults over the age of 18 who are
primarily Latino. Non-native English speakers learn the skills needed to improve
and integrate their basic reading, writing, listening and speaking. Grammar,
vocabulary and culturally relevant experiential learning are encouraged and
explored. In the last four years, approximately 99% of FIELD students were
Hispanic, with the next highest category being “Two or More Races.”
Approximately 70% were female, 30% male (nearly zero number of “decline to
state” or unknown). Across all participants, 99% had a goal of “Improve basic
English, Reading, or Math”, a little less than 1% had a goal of “Acquire job skills”,
and a trivial number of participants (single digits) selected a range of other
responses.
FIELD operates programs at 25 sites throughout 9 different counties in California,
at no cost to students. In addition to ESL classes, FIELD has a a fully accredited
charter high school for adults, known as Escuela Popular Instituto Campesino de
César Chavez (EPIC ) and a Conservation Corps known as Cesar Chavez
Environmental Corps (CCEC). Approximately 225 of the College’s ESL students
enrolled in EPIC High School. FIELD’s newest addition to EPIC is its Career
Technical Education (CTE) program. CTE focuses on skills training for rural
economic sectors that show promise for growth. This includes training in solar
photovoltaic system or renewable solar energy installation, renewable materials
management, wildfire prevention and suppression, early childhood education and
agriculture. FIELD’s current strategic objective is to enable 40,000 FIELD
students to become self-sufficient within California by exercising its knowhow in
building relationships with target markets no later than June 2021. Since 2018,
the Director, in collaboration with the College’s Non-Credit Director has worked
with FIELD to develop and launch non-credit CTE courses including business
and fire classes. In spring 2020, the first of these CTE courses were offered in
Kern County with a 4-course Small Business Startup, Marketing and Media
certificate and in fall 2020, a 3-course Firefighting certificated was offered to
FIELD students.
The ISA is in regards to the following non-credit courses listed below. Credit
courses are also permitted under the ISA, but are not currently offered. Up until
March 2020, all courses were face-to-face. Due to COVID, classes were
transitioned to hybrid and in the fall of 2020 classes started being offered online
via Zoom and Canvas. These approximate 6-week courses are taught off-
campus year-round at site locations in California at various intervals. In the past

-- 3 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 3)
text:
year, there were approximately 80 courses offered in each 6-week period for a
total of approximately 450 courses annually. The College’s policy on open
enrollment is published in its catalog and class schedules and courses are
advertised to the public. Students can earn a Certificate of Completion by
completing ESL Level 1, ESL Level 2, and ESL Level 3. The curriculum is up-to-
date. In 2018 to change the designation from ENGL to ESL and in the summer of
2020 DE addenda (1 for each level) were submitted for approval to enable online
classes in January 2021.
As stated in the Course Catalog, “The ESL program provides communicative
English skills in speaking, listening, reading, and writing to speakers of other
languages. This sequence of courses is designed to take students from survival
level English skills to high intermediate English proficiency, to prepare students
for a variety of vocational or career fields and/or for transition into credit-bearing
college courses. As a Noncredit program, the ESL Certificate of Completion is
designed to meet the needs of diverse second language learners at their own
pace.” These classes prepare students for success in the workforce and/or for
entry level credit-bearing courses, career and technical certificate programs, and
transfer programs. All courses offered by FIELD are non-credit, open entry-open
exit courses.
ESL Classes Offered from 2016-2020:
ESL 0561: ESL Level 1 (0 units, 90 hours)
ESL 0562: ESL Level 2 (0 units, 90 hours)
ESL 0563: ESL Level 3 (0 units, 90 hours)
Certificate of Completion Requirements: Complete ESL Level 1, 2, and 3 (270
total certificate hours)
The ESL Program Outcomes are for students to be able to:

-- 4 of 19 --

• Recognize and utilize basic structures and forms of English language in
speaking and writing.
• Compose grammatically correct sentences, using correct verb forms of both
regular and irregular verbs and modals.
• Recognize common etiquette forms and idiomatic expressions and utilize
appropriate language responses to questions and in common social
contexts.
• Demonstrate cultural awareness by explaining or comparing variouscultural
perspectives.
• Discuss and answer questions to a short reading selection.
• Employ appropriate vocabulary to life-skill topics, in real-life situations, and in
obtaining employment; and apply effective workplace
communicationskills.
• Demonstrate progress in pronunciation skills.
CTE Classes Offered in 2020:
Small Business Startup, Marketing and Media Courses: This 4-class
sequence was offered only once from January-June 2020. 18 out of 19 students
earned the certificate.
BA 505: Strategic Thinking, Decision Making and problem solving (0 units, 32-36
hours)
WORK 0512: Small Business Marketing, Sales & Social Media- Graphic Design
(0 units, 32-36 hours)
CSCI 050: Beginning Computer Skills (0 units, 32-36 hours)
BA 504: Communication in the Workplace (0 units, 32-36 hours)

-- 5 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 4)
text:
Certificate of Completion Requirements: Complete all 4 Small Business Courses
(128 hours)
Firefighting I Certificate Courses:
This 3-class sequence -- offered only once) started in October 2020 and will end
in January 2021. (Certificate data pending as final Fire class class offered in
January 2021 was in session when this report was submitted).
WORK532 S-190 Intro/Wildland Fire Behavior (0 units, 8 hours)
WORK533 Confined Space Awareness (0 units, 9 hours)
WORK536 Emergency Medical Responder (0 units, 54 hours)
Certificate of Completion Requirements: Complete all 3 Fire Courses (71 hours)
*The Director collaborated with the College’s Emergency Medical Services
faculty to ensure students will receive industry certificates in addition to College
certificate.
2. External Factors with Significant Impact:
a) Budgetary Constraints & Lack of Technology:
One issue instructors have relayed through SLO surveys is a lack of technology
in some of FIELD’s classrooms. While the Director has noted this issue, it is a
difficult one to tackle as many of FIELD’s sites don’t have internet
access, are also used by other entities, and some locations periodically change
throughout the year. However, students can access Mango – an online
language-learning program provided by the College’s
library – from anywhere on personal devices. In collaboration with COS’
Librarian, the Director is currently considering an alternative online language
acquisition program, which provides far more practice activities. Also, starting in

-- 6 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 5)
text:
the 2019-2020 ISA, the Director included language stipulating minimum
resources required in classrooms. “FIELD will ensure classrooms are sufficiently
equipped with learning resources throughout duration of classes. Minimum
requirements are: a standard-sized whiteboard or chalk board, one computer,
internet (when possible), a projector, and Mango access. Students will have all
necessary resources for in-class learning including materials to write on (paper or
mini-whiteboard) and handouts.” The Director continues to consider how to bring
more technology to FIELD classrooms, however the silver lining of the pandemic
was FIELD’s transition to classes conducted via Zoom, which prompted FIELD to
obtain Internet hot spots for its students. In summer 2020, DE addenda were
submitted to enable FIELD to offer ESL classes online and they should be
launched in January 2021. Once the pandemic ends, FIELD plans to have
student learning centers to support online learners and to address technology
gaps, which is explained in further detail in section 9 of this report.
b) Permission From Other Institutions: Every year the College must obtain
permission to operate in each college district in which FIELD operates as
required by Title 5 requirements, which creates an unpredictable situation. Over
the last 4 years, some colleges have understandably declined permissions due to
the need to build their own ESL programs. In the last 4 years permissions from
various districts have decreased to where currently the only college granting
permission is Bakersfield College. Past permissions have included Hartnell
College, College of the Sequoias, Cerro Coso Community College, Antelope
Valley College, and Porterville College.
c) Teaching 3 ESL Levels Concurrently: FIELD instructors report in SLO
surveys that it is challenging to teach three concurrent ESL levels (ESL 1, 2, & 3)
due to the students’ varying English speaking abilities and academic skills. The
Director initially began addressing the issue by providing FIELD’s site
coordinators some online articles, videos, and a webinar regarding multilevel
classrooms to provide as support for instructors. The Director has orchestrated
two CALPRO (California Adult Literacy Professional Development Project)
trainings on teaching in multilevel classrooms as well as an ESL workshop. In
addition, FIELD is exploring potential development of online training for new
instructors. With the imminent launche of online ESL classes (likely mid-January
2021), the issue of teaching concurrent levels will be mitigated through online
classes though it will remain as an issue for face-to-face classes and for which
the Director will remain mindful.

-- 7 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 6)
text:
d) Student Education Levels: As FIELD recruits low-skilled workers —
predominantly farmworkers — for its ESL classes, low-education levels and
literacy rates are in issue within the ESL student populations. Also, many
students either lack technology know-how or sufficient technology. This poses a
unique set of challenges to ESL instructors, challenges that should always be
factored in when developing new efforts. For example, for online classes the
Director recommends FIELD offer a pre-online training to onboard students to
ensure have they basic computer knowledge necessary to navigate the course.
e) Instructor Turnover: Due to all three of the aforementioned factors, instructor
turnover is problematic. Due to budgetary constraints, FIELD pays its instructors
less than the average Adult Education hourly wage so the pay is not competitive.
Also, instructors are generally expected to work year round with significantly less
breaks than a teacher or college instructor. Many instructors also work split shifts
requiring them to work both in the morning and the evening, 5 days a week,
almost year round. The Director has consistently advocated for more instructor
support and in the last two years FIELD has had an ESL coach to support
instructors. The Director has also discussed with staff the possibility of more
breaks, however FIELD has yet to make any changes. This is a significant matter
as many dedicated instructors have reported to the Director that though they love
their job it’s not sustainable to continue to work with so few breaks in the
schedule. The Director will continue working to support instructors to improve
instructor retention. While FIELD’s mission is noble, a mission alone doesn’t
sustain instructors. Several FIELD staff members have expressed frustration with
their perception that FIELD’s mission doesn’t match its treatment of its own staff.
3. Progress on Outcome Assessment:
a) Student Learning Outcome Surveys: In summer 2017, the Director created
a protocol and system to collect SLOs as SLOs were not previously collected
since the 2012 commencement of the ISA. In coordination with FIELD staff, the
Director collects SLOs at the end of each 6-week session, which run year-
round. Currently data is collected via SurveyMonkey and stored digitally. The
data helps inform the Director how to better support ESL students and FIELD
instructors. In the coming year, the Director anticipates utilizing eLumen for
reporting of SLOs depending on when the College implements its plan
(potentially 2021).

-- 8 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 7)
text:
CASAS Testing: The Director also collects the annual results of testing FIELD
conducts in all its ESL classes called the Comprehensive Adult Student
Assessment System (CASAS), which provides testing of basic and academic
skills for youth and adults and curriculum tools to design instruction. Every new
ESL student is given an appraisal test, and then a Pre-Test based on that
appraisal. All students who have taken a pre-test and who have attended a
minimum of 40 hours of instruction then take the post-test at the end of that
session. This is repeated for every session. Once a student takes the initial
pre-test, he or she then only takes the end of session post test from that time on
regardless of how may subsequent sessions the student attends. Below is the
CASAS data for the last 4 years.
b) Program/Course or Instructional Improvements Based on Assessment:
The Director works closely with FIELD staff to ensure quality non-credit class
offerings and sufficient oversight as all classes are provided offsite in central and
southern California.
New Textbook: In 2018, FIELD implemented a new ESL textbook series,
Ventures, based on recommendations from the CALPRO trainer who led
workshops on multi-level teaching. Ventures is a six-level, standards based,
integrated skills series for Adult Education, aligned to WIOA, NRS, English
Language Proficiency, and College and Career Readiness standards. The
textbook series provides an array or resources for both instructors and students:
Two-page lessons are designed for an hour of classroom instruction. Culture
notes and speaking, reading, and writing tips enrich and support exercises.
Review units include sections focusing on pronunciation. Each student's book
includes an audio CD, which contains 10 topical units composed of six lessons
each.
Minimum Classroom Requirements: Starting in the 2019-2020 ISA, the
Director inserted language stipulating the minimum resources required in
classrooms. “FIELD will ensure classrooms are sufficiently equipped

-- 9 of 19 --

with learning resources throughout duration of classes. Minimum requirements
are: a standard-sized whiteboard or chalk board, one computer, internet (when
possible), a projector, and Mango access. Students will have all necessary
resources for in-class learning including materials to write on (paper or mini-
whiteboard) and handouts.”
c) Program Assessment Plan for Next 4 Years: The Director will continue to
have instructors report SLOs after each session, which will continue to be
collected and stored digitally. The Director will work with College staff to ensure
SLOs are integrated into eLumen once a system is put in place. Annual CASAS
testing data will continue to be collected. Once online classes are launched fully
online in 2021, the Director will create and implement measurements to
understand student success in face-to-face versus online classes and collaborate
with FIELD staff to ensure academic quality in both face-to-face and online
classes.
4. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
a) Course Completion Rates: Course completion rates in this section pertain to
ESL and are measured by attendance of a minimum of 90 hours.

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 8)
text:
-- 10 of 19 --

Discussion on Course Completion:
Overall program completion (measured by attendance of 90 hours or more) has
markedly increased in the last four years. From 2016-17 to 2019-20, completion
increased by 36%, which is a positive trend. This can likely be attributed to
FIELD instructors emphasizing the importance of attendance. The FIELD
Director has discussed with FIELD the issue of attendance disparity and
brainstormed ideas of how to improve attendance such as tailoring the schedule
more to students lives and shortening meeting times. For example, classes have
been offered in both 2 hour and 3 hours increments. FIELD’s student population
poses inherent challenges to robust attendance because many students work
long hours and/or seasonally. FIELD reports that attendance is primarily
impacted by harvest time and children’s school breaks. FIELD has addressed
some of these issues such as scheduling classes that mirror local school district
schedules. The FIELD Director debunked an institutional myth that FIELD must
schedule the same breaks as the College, which enabled mirroring of schedules.
With the commencement of online classes in January 2021, the FIELD Director
projects improvements in attendance due to the nature of flexibility in one’s time,

-- 11 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 9)
text:
however the FIELD Director will continue to be mindful of the obstacles online
poses to this student population and monitor attendance data.
3-Class Certificate of Completion Rates for each year: Number of students
completing the sequence (ESL 1, 2 & 3)
2016-2017 2017-2018 2018-2019 2019-2020
English as a
Second
Language -
ESL 0 0 380 2
The FIELD Director acknowledges that the Completion Rate data is not accurate
as Enrollment Services did not award ESL certificates until 2018 upon the FIELD
Director’s request and as of December 2020, certificates had yet to be awarded
for 2019-2020.
a) Course Completion Rate Goal: It is not in the FIELD Director’s purview to
set Course Completion Rate goals, however the Director consistently
collaborates with FIELD to ensure program improvement. As FIELD rolls out
online classes where ESL levels are taught individually rather than concurrently,
the FIELD Director believes that proficiency will improve in ESL 3 and will be
reflected in future CASAS testing data and course completion rates.
b) Course Success Rate: As SLOs are currently not entered into a database, it
is not possible to provide a an assessment of Course Success Rate based on
SLOs. As course completion is based on positive attendance,the FIELD Director
contends that analyzing the number of students who attended minimum required
hours (90 hours for each ESL level) is not a meaningful data to analyze in terms
of course success rates. To understand student success, the CASAS data is a
far more meaningful data regarding course success as profiency in English
speaking and writing skills are assessed, which CASAS terms “Educational
Functioning Level Completion Rates” which pertains to those students who
attend more than 40 hours of instruction within the 90-hour session and have
taken a pre-test and post-test at session’s end. This following is based on
CASAS data of which the entirety can be found in section 3 of this report.

-- 12 of 19 --

-- 13 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 10)
text:
ESL 3: 37%
*State Average & Goal Data Unavailable
Conclusions from CASAS Data: As ESL level increases, average proficiency
decreases within the ESL program. This inverse relationship between ESL level
increase and proficiency decrease is consistent with state averages as seen
above. However, these ESL students tend demonstrate proficiency lower than
state averages, which is a concern to the FIELD Director, an issue the Director
brought to the College President in 2019 and to FIELD leadership. In 2019, the
FIELD Director noted significant improvements in proficiency at both the ESL 1
and ESL 2 levels from 2017-18 to 2018-19. One factor that may be attributed to
lower average proficiency than state averages, is the low-education levels and
literacy rates of as FIELD recruits low-skilled workers — predominantly
farmworkers — for its ESL classes. Also, many students either lack technology
know-how or sufficient technology. This poses a unique set of challenges to ESL
instructors, challenges that should always be factored in when developing new
efforts. Another factor may be high instructor turnover, which can be attributed to
lack of competitive pay and work schedules.
c) Faculty Ratio -- Part-Time to Full-Time Ratio: All ESL faculty are the
employees of FIELD. The College doesn’t have any control over whether FIELD
hires instructors full-time or part-time. However, FIELD has indicated that when
classes are transitioned to fully online in January 2021, those instructors will
likely be offered full-time positions. The FIELD Director believes this will help
retain instructors and will monitor turnover.
5. Qualitative Data:
Enrollments by Student & Course
(students can potentially enroll multiples times in the same course)

-- 14 of 19 --

6. Evaluation:
ISA Programs Strengths:
• Significantly bolsters College’s ability to serve diverse populations
• Supports equity populations specifically Latino students
• FIELD’s dedicated faculty
• ESL serves as a bridge to high school completion

-- 15 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 11)
text:
• FIELD organization’s resilence and adaptability to pandemic
Weaknesses:
• Attendance disparity (attendance ranges from 18-85% in individual ESL
classes)
• Number of students earning ESL certificate
• Access to student services due to proximity of FIELD classes to Weed Campus
• Inability to effectively utilize College faculty as a resource due to political
tensions
• No full-time instructors due to FIELD’s budget constraints (however this may
change in the near-future in the transition to online classes)
• High instructor turnover
Challenges
• Low-education level of students
• Student work schedules
• Student lack of technology access
• Instructor pay and schedules
7. Vision: This section is not technically applicable as the COS-FIELD ISA is
not a “program.” However the Director plans to ensure all systems are in place to
ensure student success and compliance with all Title 5 requirements pertaining
to the ISA.
8. Progress on Prior Goals: This section is not applicable as a Program
Review was never previously conducted. However, the Director affirms that all
the Recommendations from the 2016 accreditation process are met. Those
pertaining to the ISA are as follows:
Recommendation 4: In order to meet the Standard, the College should file a
Substantive Change Report regarding its Instructional Service Agreements for
the FIELD and SFPA programs. Requirement Met: In 2017, the Director wrote a
Substantive Change Report, which was approved by the ACCJC in 2017.

-- 16 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 12)
text:
Recommendation 6: In order to meet the Standard, the team recommends the
College develop mechanisms to assure that student learning outcomes
assessment and program review take place for the FIELD and SFPA programs.
Requirements Met: As previously stated, in summer 2017, the Director created a
protocol and system to collect SLOs as SLOs were not previously collected since
the 2012 commencement of the FIELD ISA. (See preceding section 3 for more
details.) Writing and submission of this 2020 Program Review meets also meets
Recommendation 6.
9. Four-Year Action Plan: In the next four years, the Director will continue
to serve as a liaison between FIELD and the College to ensure compliance with
Title 5 requirements. The Director will also continue to work closely with FIELD
staff and the College’s Non-Credit Director to build CTE pathways such as
business and firefighting. In the last three years, the Director has created
protocols and systems to help ensure compliance in such areas as:
Class Visitations: The Director established a plan to ensure there are consistent
classes visitations. Each semester, the Director visits class sites. Post-pandemic,
the Director devised a system to visit each instructor’s class for each session via
Zoom.
Instructor Evaluations: The Director created a protocol and evaluation system
to ensure FIELD instructors are evaluated by peers, students, and self as
required by the College. The Director will ensure the instructor evaluations will
continue within the online teaching environment.
Syllabi: The Director provides training to instructors regarding creating syllabi
that meet accreditation standards and collects syllabi for all sessions.
In the coming years, the Director will develop protocols and system to ensure
compliance for online classes such as:
Online ESL: With the fall 2020 approval of online ESL, the issue of teaching
concurrent levels will be mitigated through online classes. Instructors will create
individual Canvas shells for each of the three levels, which removes the
challenges associated with multi-level instruction.

-- 17 of 19 --

title: 2020 FIELD Comprehensive Program Review (Part 13)
text:
Ensure Establishment of Support Centers for Online Classes: Support
Centers will be places students go to get help with technological support
provided by teaching assistants. With the understanding that most FIELD
students have limited computer skills, teaching assistants will help with such
tasks as logging into class. There will be about 10-15 laptops in each center.
Most of the centers will be at existing FIELD locations.
The ESL instructor/s will be available online only and will offer weekly live
sessions (office hours) for students to ask questions. These live sessions won’t
be mandatory for students. Some of the class will be taught through previously
recorded video lectures. There will be a cap of about 25-30 students per course.
Due to students’ limited computer skills, the Director has suggested for FIELD
offer a pre-online training to onboard students so they have they basic computer
knowledge necessary to navigate the course. The ISA (either a separate one
covering online classes or an MOU addendum) will need to include exactly what
activities will take place in the Student Support Centers.The Agreement should
also make clear the responsibilities of each party (FIELD and COS) in providing
these online classes and services offered in the student centers.
Ensure Online Support of Students During the Pandemic: At the time this report
was written, the pandemic is underway and thus establishment of Student Center
is not feasible. However, the FIELD Director is collaborating with a COS adjunct
faculty and FIELD collegue to ensure students will be provided support ot
prepare them to use Canvas. Also, additional hours for instructor contact (12
hours per class in each ESL level) were added to the online classes, which can
be found as an attachment in the Course Outline.

-- 18 of 19 --

-- 19 of 19 --
